title: Context
description: Request context, response helpers, cookies and headers

blocks.text: Context is created fresh for every request. It carries the request, matched route, state, and all response helpers. Every middleware, layout, and page handler receives one.

import type { Context } from "@hushkey/howl";

// Key properties
ctx.url        // URL object
ctx.req        // Request
ctx.params     // { [key: string]: string }
ctx.state      // your State type
ctx.headers    // mutable response Headers
ctx.cookies    // CookieManager
ctx.isPartial  // true if SPA nav request
ctx.route      // matched route pattern
ctx.info       // Deno.ServeHandlerInfo (remote addr)
All response helpers automatically merge ctx.headers, so any cookies or headers you set in middleware will be present in the response.
Set response headers via ctx.headers. These are merged into every response the current request produces.
Default cookie options are httpOnly: true, sameSite: Strict, path: /. Override any of these per cookie.
ctx.stream() returns a raw streaming Response. Use it when you need full control over a byte stream.
For Server-Sent Events, use ctx.sse() — it handles framing, content-type, and keep-alive automatically. See the SSE section for details.

Throwing an HttpError from any handler or middleware short-circuits to a typed error response. The class is also re-exported from @hushkey/howl/runtime, so client code can detect it via instanceof HttpError without pulling server-only imports.

The message argument is optional. When omitted, the server's default error handler fills in the canonical status text (STATUS_TEXT[status]). Keeping the default off the constructor means client bundles importing HttpError from @hushkey/howl/runtime don't pull in @std/http/status.

On the client, import from @hushkey/howl/runtime: `import { HttpError } from "@hushkey/howl/runtime";`. The class is the same constructor as the server-side one, so instanceof checks work across the wire.
In middleware, call ctx.next() to invoke the next handler in the chain. Awaiting it lets you run code both before and after the downstream response.
